perturbation.original_text: Such fees and expenses will be due and payable by the later of the fifteenth (15 ) day of every month or fifteen (15) days after the date such invoice is received.
perturbation.changed_text: Such fees and expenses will be due and payable no later than sixty (60) days after the date such invoice is received.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to allow for payment 60 days after the invoice is received could violate state prompt payment laws if the company is based in a state with stricter regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota law requires prompt payment of wages. While this contract pertains to service fees rather than wages, unduly delaying payments could be construed as a violation if the contractor is economically dependent on these payments or if state law provides broader protections. The statute requires payments to be made at least every 30 days.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 181.13 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes LABOR, INDUSTRY Chapter 181 Section 181.13 181.12 181.14 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 181 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 181.13 Version List Recent History 2013 181.13 Amended 2013 c 27 s 1 1997 181.13 Amended 1997 c 83 s 5 181.13 PENALTY FOR FAILURE TO PAY WAGES PROMPTLY. (a) When any employer employing labor within this state discharges an employee, the wages or commissions actually earned and unpaid at the time of the discharge are immediately due and payable upon demand of the employee. Wages are actually earned and unpaid if the employee was not paid for all time worked at the employee's regular rate of pay or at the rate required by law, including any applicable statute, regulation, rule, ordinance, government resolution or policy, contract, or other legal authority, whichever rate of pay is greater. If the employee's earned wages and commissions are not paid within 24 hours after demand, whether the employment was by the day, hour, week, month, or piece or by commissions, the employer is in default. In addition to recovering the wages and commissions actually earned and unpaid, the discharged employee may charge and collect a penalty equal to the amount of the employee's average daily earnings at the employee's regular rate of pay or the rate required by law, whichever rate is greater, for each day up to 15 days, that the employer is in default, until full payment or other settlement, satisfactory to the discharged employee, is made. In the case of a public employer where approval of expenditures by a governing board is required, the 24-hour pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment deadline to 60 days, potentially violating Minnesota's prompt payment laws. The original text mandated payment by the 15th of each month or 15 days after invoice receipt, whichever is later. The changed text contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of prompt payment, as described in the Law Explanation, which indicates payments should be made at least every 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses penalties for failing to pay wages promptly, implying a legal expectation for timely payment. The change introduces an 'Inconsistency Legal Contradiction,' as defined, because it involves time-sensitive obligations and sets a deadline (60 days) that could violate state law regarding prompt payment.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise requested by the Company, or unless otherwise required by Governmental Rules, the Contractor shall retain, with respect to 9 each Note, for a period of 24 months from the date the Note is fully paid, all records, files and documents related to each such Note. At the end of such 24-month period, unless otherwise directed by the Company, all such items shall be transferred to the Company, or to a third party as designated by the Company, at the Company's sole cost and expense.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise requested by the Company, or unless otherwise required by Governmental Rules, the Contractor shall retain, with respect to each Note, for a period of 6 months from the date the Note is fully paid, all records, files and documents related to each such Note. At the end of such 6-month period, unless otherwise directed by the Company, all such items shall be transferred to the Company, or to a third party as designated by the Company, at the Company's sole cost and expense.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to 6 months could violate SEC rules regarding the maintenance of records related to securities offerings. Rule 17a-4 under the Securities Exchange Act specifies minimum retention periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4 requires broker-dealers to preserve certain records for specified periods, often longer than 6 months. Because this agreement is tied to a securities offering, reducing the retention period could violate federal regulations regarding record keeping for financial transactions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from 24 months to 6 months, which directly contradicts potential SEC rules requiring longer retention periods, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.17a-4, pertains to records to be preserved by brokers and dealers, and specifies a period of not less than 6 years for certain records, with the first two years easily accessible. The change to 6 months creates a clear and serious non-compliance issue related to record-keeping obligations and conflicts with federal regulations.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 60 days' prior written notice to the Contractor. The Contractor will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 90 days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 14 days' prior written notice to the Contractor. The Contractor will have the ability to terminate this Agreement by giving 14 days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 14 days could be problematic if it doesn't provide sufficient time for the contractor to wind down operations and transition responsibilities, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under Minnesota law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not a statute, the duty of good faith and fair dealing is implied in every contract under common law principles accepted in Minnesota. A 14-day termination notice might be seen as insufficient to allow a contractor to fulfill their remaining duties which could lead to a breach of this implied duty, especially if significant responsibilities transfer with the termination.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 60/90 days to 14 days for both parties. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this timeframe might be insufficient for the contractor to wind down operations, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under Minnesota law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a 14-day notice could breach the implied duty of good faith if it doesn't allow the contractor to fulfill remaining duties. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the explanations provided strongly suggest a contradiction of the implied duty of good faith. The changed text creates an inconsistency regarding the legally required timeframe for contract termination, especially regarding the contractor's ability to wind down operations. Thus, a contradiction exists.
